questions.question: TypeScript decides whether one type is assignable to another primarily by:
options: The shape (members) of the type, regardless of its declared name
The declared name, so two identically-shaped interfaces are incompatible
Whether the value's class explicitly uses an `implements` clause
A runtime reflection check performed when the assignment executes
questions.explanation: TypeScript is structurally typed: a value fits a type if it has the right members, no declared relationship required. Nominal (name-based) checks and `implements` requirements describe C#/Java, not TypeScript, and there is no runtime check because types are erased.

questions.question: You receive a value typed `unknown` from `JSON.parse`. What must you do before calling `value.toUpperCase()`?
options: Narrow `value` (e.g. with `typeof value === "string"`) so the compiler proves it is a string
Nothing — `unknown` accepts any member access, just like `any`
Cast the whole module to `any` so checking is disabled project-wide
Re-declare the variable as `void` so the result is ignored
questions.explanation: `unknown` is the safe top type: it accepts any value in but forbids member access until you narrow. That narrowing requirement is exactly what distinguishes it from `any`, which would let `value.toUpperCase()` through unchecked.

questions.question: Because TypeScript uses type erasure, `Array<string>` and `Array<number>` are the same construct at runtime and a type parameter `T` has no runtime presence.
questions.explanation: Types are stripped at emit, so generics are not reified (unlike C#). At runtime both are just `Array`, and there is no way to inspect `T` — the single biggest gotcha for developers expecting reflection.

questions.question: Which return type best describes a function that always throws and therefore never returns normally?
options: `never`
`void`
`undefined`
`unknown`
questions.explanation: `never` is the bottom type for code paths that never produce a value (always throw or loop forever). `void` means the function does return but with no usable value, and `undefined` is a specific returned value.

questions.question: For object types, how do `A | B` (union) and `A & B` (intersection) compare in terms of guaranteed members?
options: The union guarantees only the common members; the intersection requires all members of both
The union requires all members of both; the intersection guarantees only the common members
Both guarantee exactly the same set of members in all cases
The union guarantees all members of both, just with each marked optional
questions.explanation: A union value is one-of, so only members common to every member are safely accessible without narrowing; an intersection value is all-of, requiring every member of both. The member sets move opposite to object size, which trips people up.

questions.question: Given `type Opts = { id: number }` and `declare function run(o: Opts): void`, why does `run({ id: 1, extra: true })` error while assigning the same object to a variable first (`const o = { id: 1, extra: true }; run(o)`) compiles?
options: Line A is a fresh object literal, which triggers the excess-property check; once stored in a variable the literal is no longer fresh, so plain structural compatibility applies
Line A fails because `extra: true` makes the object structurally incompatible with `Opts`; the variable version coerces the type
Line B errors too — both lines are rejected for the unknown `extra` property
Line A errors because object literals cannot be passed to functions without a type annotation
questions.explanation: Excess-property checks fire only on fresh literals passed directly. Storing the object in a variable removes freshness, so structural compatibility (which permits extra members) takes over. The object is structurally compatible with `Opts` in both cases.

questions.question: An array method expects a callback typed `(x: T) => void`, yet passing `array.push` (which returns a `number`) compiles without error. Why?
options: A `void` return type means the result is ignored, so a function returning a value is still assignable to that slot
`push` is special-cased by the compiler to be callback-compatible
The compiler widens the callback's return type to `number` to match `push`
`void` and `number` are mutually assignable, so any return type fits
questions.explanation: The `void`-return rule says callers must ignore the result, so a function that returns something is assignable where `() => void` is expected. This is exactly what lets `array.push` be passed to `forEach`. `void` and `number` are not generally interchangeable.

questions.question: What is the type `X`?
questions.code:
const arr = ["a", "b", "c"] as const;
type X = (typeof arr)[number];
options: `string`
`string[]`
`readonly ["a", "b", "c"]`
questions.explanation: `as const` gives `arr` the type `readonly ["a", "b", "c"]`; indexing a tuple type with `number` yields the union of its element types. This is the standard idiom for deriving a union from an array of literals. Without `as const`, the elements would widen to `string`.

questions.question: What is the type of `b.method` versus `a.method`?
questions.code:
const a = { method: "GET" } as { method: "GET" | "POST" };
const b = { method: "GET" } satisfies { method: "GET" | "POST" };
options: `b.method` is `"GET"`; `a.method` is `"GET" | "POST"`
Both `a.method` and `b.method` are `"GET"`
Both `a.method` and `b.method` are `"GET" | "POST"`
`b.method` is `"GET" | "POST"`; `a.method` is `"GET"`
questions.explanation: `satisfies` validates the shape but returns the original narrow inferred type, so `b.method` stays the literal `"GET"`. The assertion `as` replaces the inferred type with the broader declared one, widening `a.method` to `"GET" | "POST"`.

questions.question: Which statements about discriminated unions and the `never`-based exhaustiveness check are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Each member sharing a common literal-typed tag (e.g. `kind`) lets the compiler narrow on a `switch`
Assigning the switched value to a `never` in the `default` branch makes the compiler error if a new member is added but unhandled
The exhaustiveness guarantee from `never` is enforced at compile time, not just at runtime
An ordinary shared property of a wider type (e.g. `id: number`) can drive the same narrowing as a literal discriminant
A runtime `default: throw` alone provides the same compile-time exhaustiveness guarantee as the `never` pattern
questions.explanation: A literal-typed discriminant enables narrowing and, combined with a `never` default, gives a compile-time error when a new variant is unhandled. A non-literal shared property (option 4) cannot drive narrowing, and a runtime `throw` (option 5) only fails at runtime, not at compile time.

questions.question: Which statements correctly contrast TypeScript's `private` modifier with ECMAScript `#private` fields? (Select all that apply.)
options: `private` is a compile-time visibility check only and is erased at runtime
`#name` is enforced by the JavaScript engine and throws on outside access
A `private` member is still readable at runtime (e.g. via `Object.keys` or JSON)
`#`-fields participate in TypeScript's structural type compatibility just like `private` members
Both `private` and `#` give identical, runtime-enforced encapsulation
questions.explanation: `private` is soft, compile-time-only privacy that disappears at runtime and is readable; `#name` is hard, engine-enforced privacy. Hash-private fields are branded and not part of the structural type (so option 4 is wrong), and the two are not equivalent (option 5 is wrong).

questions.question: What is `Result`?
questions.code:
type ToArray<T> = T extends unknown ? T[] : never;
type Result = ToArray<string | number>;
options: `string[] | number[]`
`(string | number)[]`
`never`
`Array<string | number>` which equals `string[]` only
questions.explanation: Because the checked type is a naked (bare) type parameter, the conditional distributes over each union member: `ToArray<string> | ToArray<number>` = `string[] | number[]`. To get `(string | number)[]` you would wrap the parameter in a tuple: `[T] extends [unknown] ? T[] : never`.

questions.question: Unlike `Pick<T, K>`, whose `K` is constrained to `keyof T`, the key argument of `Omit<T, K>` is not constrained to `keyof T`, so a misspelled key passed to `Omit` silently no-ops instead of erroring.
questions.explanation: This asymmetry is a real gotcha: `Pick`'s `K` is checked against `keyof T` (a typo errors), but `Omit`'s key parameter is not, so `Omit<User, "passwrod">` compiles and removes nothing.

questions.question: Which of the following are correct defaults or behaviors for a fresh tsconfig under TypeScript 6.0? (Select all that apply.)
options: `strict` is `true` by default
`esModuleInterop` is always on and can no longer be disabled
`types` defaults to `[]`, so you must list `@types` packages explicitly to include them globally
`module` defaults to `commonjs` and `target` defaults to `es5`
`noUncheckedIndexedAccess` is enabled automatically as part of `strict`
questions.explanation: TS 6.0 ships `strict: true`, always-on `esModuleInterop`, and `types: []` by default. `module` defaults to `esnext` and `target` to `es2025` (not commonjs/es5), and `noUncheckedIndexedAccess` is a separate option not included in `strict`.
